title: 09 - General Pathology of Infectious Diseases - Flashcards
description: Third-party generated Chapter 9 flashcards optimized for active recall, spaced repetition, and exam revision. The deck is organized around microbial classes, diagnostics, transmission, pathogenesis, immune evasion, and morphologic response patterns.

disclaimer.endorsement_notice: These flashcards are third-party generated study materials. They are not produced by, reviewed by, approved by, endorsed by, or affiliated with the textbook authors, Elsevier, the Royal College of Pathologists of Australasia, or any other authority, institution, publisher, or examining body.
disclaimer.use_note: Use these flashcards as revision support alongside the original source material.

source_notes.source_title: Robbins Basic Pathology, 10th Edition, Chapter 9: General Pathology of Infectious Diseases
RCPA - Basic Pathological Sciences Syllabus 2026 - October 2025
source_notes.organization: Royal College of Pathologists of Australasia
source_notes.reference_note: Used for topic emphasis, exam framing, and study-bucket structure.

source_notes.source_title: Chapter 9 Study Notes and Exam Prep Questions created from the approved Chapter 9 source set
source_notes.reference_note: Used to compress Chapter 9 into single-fact active-recall prompts.

deck.deck_name: Pathology::Chapter 09 - General Pathology of Infectious Diseases
topic_buckets: Routes of entry, spread, and transmission
Viral and bacterial mechanisms of injury
deck.design_note: Cards are kept mostly single-fact and under one question per card for rapid recall.
deck.page_reference_note: The source_page_refs arrays use the printed book page number from Robbins Basic Pathology 10th Edition, not the sequential PDF page position. Chapter 9 occupies book pages 341-360.

cards.front: What is a prion?
cards.back: A misfolded form of host prion protein that can transmit disease without nucleic acid.

cards.front: What property of abnormal PrP makes prion diseases self-propagating?
cards.back: Protease resistance combined with the ability to convert normal PrP into the abnormal form.

cards.front: What is the defining lifestyle of viruses?
cards.back: They are obligate intracellular parasites that rely on host metabolic machinery for replication.

cards.front: What makes bacteria prokaryotes?
cards.back: They lack membrane-bound nuclei and other membrane-enclosed organelles.

cards.front: What distinctive outer-membrane component makes gram-negative bacteria important triggers of septic shock?
cards.back: Lipopolysaccharide (LPS).

cards.front: Which bacterial genera are notable for lacking a cell wall?
cards.back: Mycoplasma and Ureaplasma.

cards.front: What does thermal dimorphism mean in medical mycology?
cards.back: Growth as hyphae at room temperature and as yeast at body temperature.

cards.front: In many helminth infections, what mainly determines disease severity?
cards.back: The number of infecting organisms and the host inflammatory response to their eggs or larvae.

cards.front: What is the microbiome?
cards.back: The diverse population of bacteria, fungi, and viruses that live in or on the human body.

cards.front: What is one major protective role of the intestinal microbiome?
cards.back: It supports epithelial integrity and competitively inhibits colonization by pathogenic microbes.

cards.front: What does dysbiosis mean?
cards.back: A disease-associated change in the composition of the microbiome.

cards.front: Why do broad-spectrum antibiotics predispose to C. difficile infection?
cards.back: They suppress normal commensal flora and allow toxin-producing C. difficile to overgrow.

cards.front: What is the preferred test for HSV encephalitis in CSF?
cards.back: PCR-based nucleic acid testing.

cards.front: What class of organisms is identified by acid-fast staining?
cards.back: Mycobacteria, and modified acid-fast stains can also help with nocardiae.

cards.front: Mucicarmine staining is a classic clue for which pathogen?
cards.back: Cryptococcus.

cards.front: What major diagnostic limitation does mass spectrometry have in clinical microbiology?
cards.back: It can identify organisms rapidly but does not determine antibiotic sensitivity.

cards.front: What is one major source of newly emerging human pathogens?
cards.back: Animal reservoirs that transmit zoonotic infections to humans.

cards.front: What genetic event made the 2011 outbreak E. coli strain more virulent?
cards.back: Acquisition of a shiga-toxin gene from a bacteriophage.

cards.front: What human behavior helped SARS spread rapidly to multiple countries?
cards.back: International air travel.

cards.front: Which mosquito genus is the main vector for Zika virus?
cards.back: Aedes.

cards.front: What congenital defect is classically linked to Zika infection during pregnancy?
cards.back: Microcephaly.

cards.bucket: Routes of entry, spread, and transmission
cards.front: What are the two key innate defenses of normal skin against infection?
cards.back: A tough keratinized barrier and a low-pH surface with inhibitory fatty acids.

cards.bucket: Routes of entry, spread, and transmission
cards.front: What is the usual route by which urinary tract infections ascend into the bladder?
cards.back: Entry from the exterior through the urethra.

cards.bucket: Routes of entry, spread, and transmission
cards.front: What keeps vaginal pH low between puberty and menopause?
cards.back: Lactobacilli metabolizing glycogen in the vaginal epithelium.

cards.bucket: Routes of entry, spread, and transmission
cards.front: Which intestinal cells are exploited by several pathogens to cross from the lumen into mucosal lymphoid tissue?
cards.back: M cells.

cards.bucket: Routes of entry, spread, and transmission
cards.front: Why does Vibrio cholerae usually require a large infectious dose in healthy people?
cards.back: Normal gastric acid kills most of the ingested organisms.

cards.bucket: Routes of entry, spread, and transmission
cards.front: How do influenza viruses attach to respiratory epithelial cells?
cards.back: Hemagglutinin on the viral surface binds sialic acid on host cells.

cards.bucket: Routes of entry, spread, and transmission
cards.front: How does rabies virus travel from a bite site to the CNS?
cards.back: By retrograde transport along nerves.

cards.bucket: Routes of entry, spread, and transmission
cards.front: What are the three main routes by which microbes disseminate within the body?
cards.back: Through lymphatics, through blood, and along nerves.

cards.bucket: Viral and bacterial mechanisms of injury
cards.front: What most directly determines viral tropism at the cell surface?
cards.back: The presence of the specific host receptor needed for viral binding and entry.

cards.bucket: Viral and bacterial mechanisms of injury
cards.front: What physical feature helps confine rhinovirus to the upper respiratory tract?
cards.back: It replicates best at the cooler temperature of the upper airway.

cards.bucket: Viral and bacterial mechanisms of injury
cards.front: What is the main mechanism of hepatocyte injury in hepatitis B infection?
cards.back: Cytotoxic T-cell-mediated destruction of infected hepatocytes.

cards.bucket: Viral and bacterial mechanisms of injury
cards.front: What are bacterial pathogenicity islands?
cards.back: Clusters of virulence genes that help determine whether a bacterium can cause disease.

cards.bucket: Viral and bacterial mechanisms of injury
cards.front: What is quorum sensing?
cards.back: Coordinated regulation of bacterial gene expression when population density becomes high.

cards.bucket: Viral and bacterial mechanisms of injury
cards.front: Why are biofilms clinically important?
cards.back: They shield bacteria from immune mechanisms and increase resistance to antimicrobial drugs.

cards.bucket: Viral and bacterial mechanisms of injury
cards.front: What are bacterial adhesins?
cards.back: Surface molecules that bind host cells or extracellular matrix and help bacteria colonize tissues.

cards.bucket: Viral and bacterial mechanisms of injury
cards.front: Which innate immune receptor pathway is activated by lipid A of LPS?
cards.back: CD14 with TLR4 signaling.

cards.bucket: Viral and bacterial mechanisms of injury
cards.front: What is the main action of anthrax edema factor?
cards.back: It acts as a calmodulin-dependent adenylate cyclase that raises intracellular cAMP and causes edema.

cards.front: What is antigenic variation?
cards.back: Microbial alteration of surface antigens to evade preexisting immune recognition.

cards.front: How does a bacterial capsule increase virulence?
cards.back: It inhibits phagocytosis.

cards.front: How does Mycobacterium tuberculosis survive after macrophage phagocytosis?
cards.back: It blocks phagosome-lysosome fusion.

cards.front: What is a superantigen?
cards.back: A toxin that activates large numbers of T cells nonspecifically and triggers massive cytokine release.

cards.front: What immune mechanism causes poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis?
cards.back: Deposition of immune complexes in glomeruli.

cards.front: What host response produces tissue injury and caseation in tuberculosis?
cards.back: T-cell-mediated delayed hypersensitivity with granulomatous inflammation.

cards.front: How can herpesviruses reduce CD8+ T-cell recognition of infected cells?
cards.back: By interfering with MHC class I antigen presentation.

cards.front: What histologic pattern is most typical of pyogenic bacterial infection?
cards.back: Suppurative inflammation rich in neutrophils and pus.

cards.front: What histologic pattern is classically produced by Mycobacterium tuberculosis and some fungi?
cards.back: Granulomatous inflammation.

cards.front: What pattern of tissue response is typical of many viral infections such as HPV or herpesvirus?
cards.back: A cytopathic-cytoproliferative reaction.

cards.front: What pathologic pattern dominates clostridial myonecrosis?
cards.back: Rapid toxin-mediated tissue necrosis with relatively little inflammation.

cards.front: Antibody deficiency most strongly predisposes to severe infection by which pathogens?
cards.back: Extracellular bacteria, especially encapsulated organisms.

cards.front: T-cell defects most strongly predispose to which pathogens?
cards.back: Intracellular pathogens, especially viruses and some parasites.

cards.front: Deficiency of the late complement components predisposes to infection with what genus?
cards.back: Neisseria.

cards.front: Defects in neutrophil function predispose to infections with what groups of organisms?
cards.back: Staphylococcus aureus, some gram-negative bacteria, and fungi.

cards.front: Inherited defects in IL-17 immunity, including some STAT3 abnormalities, are associated with what infection pattern?
cards.back: Chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis.
